heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO XIII
card_summary: This chunk continues Campanini's biographical introduction to Francesco Zorzi, covering the period roughly 1504–1530. It details Zorzi's role as confessor to the Clarisse convent of the Holy Sepulchre in Venice, where he witnessed and recorded the mystical phenomena of suor Chiara Bugni, comparing her to Clare of Assisi and citing a passage from the De harmonia mundi describing her Eucharistic trance over Holy Week. The section also covers Zorzi's repeated candidacy for the Venetian patriarchate, his diplomatic and intelligence activities for the Republic, his role in the 1517 Araceli chapter that finalized the Franciscan Observant–Conventual split, the composition timeline of De harmonia mundi (established by two internal date-references to 1520 and 1523, including a passage on Julian calendar reform discussed at the Fifth Lateran Council), his 1528 conversion and baptism of a Neapolitan Jewish rabbi in San Marco, and his early involvement in Henry VIII's divorce case from 1530.
essay:
## Overview

Pages 13–17 of Campanini's *Saggio introduttivo* present a dense biographical narrative of Francesco Zorzi from approximately 1504 to the early 1530s. Rather than theological analysis, the focus is on the institutional, diplomatic, and compositional history behind the *De harmonia mundi* (1525), situating the work within the concrete pressures of Venetian religious and political life.

---

## The Mystical Phenomena at the Convent of the Holy Sepulchre (from 1504)

From 1504 Zorzi served as confessor and spiritual director to the Clarisse convent of the Holy Sepulchre in Venice. The central figure here is **suor Chiara Bugni**, abbess of the convent, who was the subject of extraordinary prodigies: a metal plant flowering and a personal apparition of Christ who presented her with a chalice containing his blood. Zorzi kept a diary of these miraculous events, a copy of which survives in the archive of S. Francesco della Vigna.

Campanini notes that Zorzi himself recalled Chiara Bugni in the *De harmonia mundi*, explicitly linking her to **Clare of Assisi**, the founder of the Clarisse order. The passage quoted directly from the *De harmonia mundi* reads:

> Chiara d'Assisi giacque dal Giovedì santo fino al giorno di Pasqua contemplando in sfere più elevate la croce: quando tornò in sé, credette di aver riposato per meno di un'ora. Ho sperimentato lo stesso prodigio nel caso di un'altra Chiara, simile alla prima per il nome e per lo stile di vita la quale, dopo aver ricevuto il sacramento della comunione alla sera del Giovedì santo, restò inginocchiata e immobile fino alla domenica, in cui, risorgendo come Cristo, mi raccontò le molte cose che aveva veduto.

<cite>p. 13–14 (quotation from *De harmonia mundi*, cited by Campanini)</cite>

This passage is significant for several reasons: it demonstrates Zorzi's direct experience with visionary mysticism, his typological reading of history (the two Claras as spiritual doubles), and the Eucharistic and Christological framing that characterizes his broader theological imagination. Notwithstanding Zorzi's sympathies, ecclesiastical authorities reacted coldly to the apparitions, prudently suppressing them, though Zorzi was permitted to retain his spiritual guidance role.

---

## Candidacies for the Venetian Patriarchate and Diplomatic Activity

Between 1504 and 1508, Zorzi was a candidate for the Venetian patriarchate on three separate occasions, never elected but consistently present at the final ballot, signaling his high standing among the Republic's governing aristocracy. He also undertook several journeys to northern Italy for Franciscan chapter meetings, during which he functioned as informal diplomat and, in some cases, as an intelligence agent for Venice. The most notable missions include:

- **1509**: Journey to Ferrara, officially for the Franciscan chapter
- **1510**: Contacts with representatives of the League of Cambrai
- **1513**: Mission to the Duke of Mantua
- **1514**: A "pastoral visit" to Valdagno with a significant political agenda

Campanini also notes Zorzi's supervisory role in the construction of a Franciscan sanctuary at Motta di Livenza, following a miraculous apparition of the Virgin — marking his first recorded involvement in architectural activity, a domain where he would later become influential (the reference anticipates his later role in S. Francesco della Vigna).

---

## Franciscan Politics: The Araceli Chapter and the Observant–Conventual Split (1517)

From 1513–1515 Zorzi held the important office of provincial vicar. He attended the *capitolo generalissimo* at the Araceli in Rome in 1517 — a watershed moment at which Pope Leo X formally ratified the separation between the **Conventuali** and the **Osservanti**. Campanini characterizes this outcome as "at least in part a victory for the Observance," in which Zorzi played a non-marginal role. He was subsequently elected *definitore generale* of the Observants and attended the general chapter at Lyons in 1518.

---

## Composition Timeline of the *De harmonia mundi*

The *De harmonia mundi* was published in 1525 by the Venetian printer **Bernardino Vitali**, dedicated to Pope Clement VII. Campanini carefully reconstructs the composition period as ca. 1519–1523 by means of two internal date-references.

**First reference** (Third Canticle, thus probably composed last):

> Ora che ci troviamo nel 1523° anno a partire dalla nascita di Cristo.

<cite>p. 15 (internal dating passage from the Third Canticle of *De harmonia mundi*, first identified by François Secret)</cite>

**Second reference** (First Canticle, suggesting an earlier compositional layer around 1520): this appears in the context of a discussion of the **Julian calendar's accumulated errors**, a topic debated at the **Fifth Lateran Council (1512–1517)** under Leo X's reform initiative. Zorzi offers his own astronomical calculation:

> Tuttavia noi abbiamo dimostrato che dall'anno della nascita di Cristo, in cui il sole entrava nel segno del Capricorno il 25 di dicembre, fino ad oggi in cui il sole entra in Capricorno intorno all'11, il sole, in 1520 anni ha anticipato il suo corso di circa quattordici giorni. Da ciò si ricava con esattezza che ogni cento anni il sole anticipa il suo corso di un giorno, spostandosi di un grado.

<cite>p. 15–16 (internal dating passage from the First Canticle of *De harmonia mundi*)</cite>

Campanini corrects an earlier error by **François Secret**, who had attempted to use a purported borrowing by the Neapolitan scholar **Benedetto di Falco** (ca. 1520) to fix a terminus ante quem, but later retracted this when the relevant work (*De origine hebraicarum litterarum*) was redated to 1541. Campanini credits Zorzi himself with providing the 1520 date directly, thus locating the beginning of the final composition around that year.

---

## The Conversion of a Jewish Rabbi (1528) and the *In Scripturam Sacram Problemata*

In 1528 Zorzi preached in the Basilica of San Marco to celebrate the baptism of a Neapolitan Jewish man named **Jacob**, described as learned in Hebrew and formerly employed as a tutor to the children of the Mantuan ambassador. Campanini quotes the eyewitness account of the chronicler **Marin Sanudo**:

> (...) Et al mezzo di la messa, fra Francesco Zorzi di l'ordine di San Francesco comenzò una predica; et questo perché uno hebreo napolitano nominato Jacob, docto in ebreo, qual è stà con l'orator di Mantoa et ha insegnato a' soi fioli, è venuto a contritione et si ha voluto redur a la fede di Christo et si batizoe sopra l'altro pulpito...

<cite>p. 17 (Marin Sanudo, *Diarii*, quoted by Campanini)</cite>

Campanini tentatively identifies this Jacob with the "famous rabbi" whom Zorzi mentions in his second major work, the *In Scripturam Sacram Problemata* (published 1536):

> ... Presso il famoso rabbino degli ebrei che io stesso iniziai alla fede cristiana, dopo che ebbe abbandonato il giudaismo.

<cite>p. 17 (quotation from *In Scripturam Sacram Problemata*, 1536)</cite>

This identification, if correct, has implications for Zorzi's Hebraic learning and the living conversionary milieu in which his Kabbalistic studies were conducted — Jacob's expertise in Hebrew would have made him a significant intellectual interlocutor.

---

## Henry VIII's Divorce (1530)

The chunk concludes mid-sentence with the opening of the episode of Henry VIII's divorce. From 1530 Zorzi was approached by the royal emissary **Richard Croke** (operating under the alias "Giovanni di Fiandra") to provide canonical arguments supporting the nullity of Henry's marriage. Zorzi apparently engaged immediately, working to assemble books, documentary evidence, and willing theologians. This episode, which brought Zorzi into pan-European religious controversy, will presumably be developed in the following chunk.

---

## Significance for the *De harmonia mundi*

This biographical section underlines that the *De harmonia mundi* was composed during years of intense Franciscan institutional conflict, diplomatic activity, mystical engagement, and Hebrew-Christian encounter. The calendar passage in the First Canticle demonstrates that Zorzi's cosmological speculations were grounded in contemporary scientific and conciliar debates, not purely in the Neoplatonic and Kabbalistic library tradition. The mystical episode of Chiara Bugni provides a lived experiential correlative to the theosophical cosmology of the main work.
